cards.text: A {{c1::member initializer list}} is a list that directly initializes member variables, appearing after the constructor parameter list with a {{c2::colon}}.

Member initializer list syntax:
ClassName(int x, int y) {{c1:::}} {{c2::m_x{x}}}, {{c3::m_y{y}}}
{
}

cards.question: Rewrite this constructor using a member initializer list:
cards.answer:
Point(int x, int y) : m_x{x}, m_y{y}
{
}
cards.explanation: Member initializer list directly initializes members instead of using assignment in the constructor body.

cards.concept: Why member initializer lists are better than assignment in constructor body
cards.explanation:
• More efficient: Initializes directly instead of default-initialize then assign
  • Required for const members: Const members must be initialized, not assigned
  • Required for references: References must be initialized
  • Required for members without default constructor: Some types can't be default-initialized
  • Clearer intent: Shows initialization vs other logic
examples: Foo(int x) { m_x = x; }  // default-init then assign (less efficient)

cards.front: Why must const members be initialized using a member initializer list?
cards.back: Const members cannot be assigned to after creation. They must be initialized at the point of creation. Assignment in the constructor body comes after initialization, so it won't work for const members.

cards.question: What's wrong with this code?
cards.answer: Cannot assign to const member m_id. Must use member initializer list: ID(int id) : m_id{id} { }
cards.explanation: Const members must be initialized using a member initializer list, not assigned in the constructor body.

cards.text: Members are initialized in the {{c1::order they are declared}} in the class, NOT the order they appear in the member initializer list.

cards.front: What is the best practice for ordering members in the initializer list?
cards.back: List members in the initializer list in the same order they are declared in the class. This prevents confusion since initialization always happens in declaration order regardless of initializer list order.

cards.text: Best practice: {{c1::Prefer member initializer lists}} over assignment in the constructor body for initializing member variables.

cards.front: Can you mix default member initializers with member initializer lists?
cards.back: Yes. Members in the initializer list use those values. Members not in the initializer list use their default member initializer. Members with neither are default-initialized.

cards.question: What values do the members have?
cards.code:
class Data
{
private:
    int m_a{1};
    int m_b{2};
    int m_c{3};
public:
    Data(int a) : m_a{a}, m_b{5}
    {
    }
};

Data d{10};
cards.answer:
m_a = 10 (from initializer list)
m_b = 5 (from initializer list)
m_c = 3 (from default member initializer)
cards.explanation: Initializer list values override default member initializers. m_c is not in the list so it uses its default value of 3.

cards.front: Can reference members be initialized using assignment in the constructor body?
cards.back: No. Like const members, reference members must be initialized using a member initializer list. References cannot be reseated after initialization.
